stem: Compute the inner product \( \mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v} \) of \( \mathbf{u} = ([[u1]], [[u2]], [[u3]], [[u4]]) \) and \( \mathbf{v} = ([[v1]], [[v2]], [[v3]], [[v4]]) \) in \( \mathbb{R}^4 \).
solution: The inner product is the sum of componentwise products: \( ([[u1]])([[v1]]) + ([[u2]])([[v2]]) + ([[u3]])([[v3]]) + ([[u4]])([[v4]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This flips the sign of the last product term.
This flips the sign of the second product term.
This adds all the components instead of summing componentwise products.
This is off by one from the correct inner product.
This is off by one from the correct inner product.
This is off by two from the correct inner product.
This is off by two from the correct inner product.
This has the wrong overall sign.

stem: What is the norm \( \|\mathbf{v}\| = \sqrt{v_1^2 + v_2^2} \) of the vector \( \mathbf{v} = ([[a]], [[b]]) \) in \( \mathbb{R}^2 \)?
solution: By construction \( v_1^2+v_2^2 \) is a perfect square: \( \|\mathbf{v}\| = \sqrt{([[a]])^2+([[b]])^2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to take the square root, leaving the squared norm.
This adds the components instead of using the Pythagorean formula.
This uses the sum of absolute values (the taxicab/\(\ell^1\) norm), not the Euclidean norm.
This is off by one from the correct norm.
This is off by one from the correct norm.
This is off by two from the correct norm.
This has the wrong sign; a norm cannot be negative.

stem: For what value of \( t \) are the vectors \( \mathbf{u} = ([[a]], [[b]], [[c]]) \) and \( \mathbf{v} = ([[d]], [[e]], t) \) orthogonal?
distractor_rationales: This has the wrong sign, missing the negative from solving for \( t \).
This forgets to divide by the coefficient \( c \) of \( t \).
This multiplies by \( c \) instead of dividing by it.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This has the wrong sign.
This is just the third coordinate of \( \mathbf{u} \), unrelated to the orthogonality condition.

stem: Let \( \mathbf{u} = ([[u1]], [[u2]], [[u3]]) \) and \( \mathbf{v} = ([[v1]], [[v2]], [[v3]]) \) in \( \mathbb{R}^3 \). By the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality, \( (\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{u})(\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{v}) - (\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v})^2 \ge 0 \). What is the value of \( (\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{u})(\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{v}) - (\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v})^2 \) for these vectors?
distractor_rationales: This has the sign of the whole expression flipped.
This forgets to subtract \( (\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v})^2 \).
This is only the squared dot product term, missing the norm product.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This has the wrong sign.
This adds the norm-squared values and subtracts the dot product without squaring or multiplying correctly.

stem: The vectors \( \mathbf{u} = ([[a1]], [[b1]]) \) and \( \mathbf{v} = ([[a2]], [[b2]]) \) in \( \mathbb{R}^2 \) satisfy \( \|\mathbf{u}\| = [[c1]] \) and \( \|\mathbf{v}\| = [[c2]] \). What is \( \cos\theta \), where \( \theta \) is the angle between \( \mathbf{u} \) and \( \mathbf{v} \)?
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by \( \|\mathbf{u}\|\|\mathbf{v}\| \), leaving just the dot product.
This divides by \( \|\mathbf{u}\| \) only, forgetting \( \|\mathbf{v}\| \).
This divides by \( \|\mathbf{v}\| \) only, forgetting \( \|\mathbf{u}\| \).
This has the wrong sign.
This uses only the first-coordinate product, dropping the second term of the dot product.
This is off by one from the correct cosine.
This is off by one from the correct cosine.
